did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach, specifically a modified Xception model with transfer learning, to classify melanoma skin cancer images from the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, including Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++, to generate heatmaps for model explainability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a computational model using a modified Xception architecture with transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (90.24% accuracy, 91.83% sensitivity, 89.89% specificity) and introduces novel contributions through the application of Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ for explainability. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICARC), it contains original results, methods, and data analysis rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Classification with Explainability
journal: 2023 3rd International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying melanoma skin cancer by developing a deep learning model that combines high performance with explainability. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, extracting 614 nevus and 614 malignant melanoma images, and applied a modified Xception model with transfer learning. To optimize the model, they employed Bayesian hyperparameter search and retained batch-normalization layers for fine-tuning. The classification performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, achieving 90.24% accuracy, 91.83% sensitivity, and 89.89% specificity. Additionally, the study introduced Explainable AI (XAI) by generating heatmaps using Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ to visualize the regions contributing to the classification decisions. The results indicate that Grad-CAM++ provided superior explainability compared to Grad-CAM, enhancing the trustworthiness of the diagnostic support model.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classification classes in the experiment. 614 images were extracted from the HAM10000 dataset. The model achieved 91.83% sensitivity for this class.

Used as the benign comparator class in the binary classification task. Described as 'not cancer'. 614 images were extracted from the HAM10000 dataset. The model achieved 89.89% specificity for this class.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma skin cancer images into 'malignant melanoma' and 'nevus' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'classify melanoma skin cancer images' using a modified Xception model on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevus). Performance was evaluated using accuracy (90.24%), sensitivity (91.83%), and specificity (89.89%). The study also employed Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ to generate heatmaps for visualizing the regions contributing to the classification decisions, but the core prediction task remains binary classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing dermoscopic and clinical images of common pigmented skin lesions, part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Challenge 2018 Task 2. The study extracted a balanced subset of 1228 images without duplicates.

use.training: Used for training the modified Xception model with transfer learning. Images were resized to 224x224 pixels and augmented with rotation and flip.
use.other: The corresponding attribute masks from the ISIC Challenge 2018 Task 2 dataset were used to validate the explainability visualization heatmaps.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To fine-tune hyperparameters for the classification model.
To evaluate the performance of Grad-CAM and Kernel SHAP by comparing generated saliency maps with ground truth masks (sanity checks for reproducibility, model dependence, and sensitivity).
To evaluate the performance of the classification model across all classification thresholds.
To calculate accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity by counting true-positive, false-positive, false-negative, and true-negative instances.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, with comparisons to other studies based on these metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.split: Test set (1228 images: 614 nevus, 614 malignant melanoma)
HAM10000.notes: The model retrained with batch normalization (unfrozen) was selected as the best performing model. The study also compared explainability using Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++, noting that Grad-CAM++ provided better coverage for regions given by attribute masks compared to Grad-CAM. The best performance was achieved with 70 epochs.

Young et al. [3].Model: InceptionV3 with 30 sub-models via Bayesian hyper-parameter search

architecture_details.base_technique: Depth-wise separable convolution and shortcuts between convolution blocks

training_details.dataset: HAM10000 (subset of 1228 images: 614 nevus, 614 malignant melanoma)
training_details.batch_normalization_handling: Lower batch-normalization layers unfrozen for retraining

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including rotation and flip
Exclusion of duplicate images from the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training images from the HAM10000 dataset. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to the training images from the HAM10000 dataset. Specific axis or probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed computational model using a modified Xception architecture with transfer learning and Bayesian hyperparameter search achieved a classification accuracy of 90.24%, sensitivity of 91.83%, and specificity of 89.89% on the HAM10000 dataset.
Retraining the model with unfrozen batch-normalization layers resulted in superior performance (90.24% accuracy) compared to freezing them (82.11% accuracy).
The model achieved an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 0.905, indicating strong performance across all classification thresholds.

The study demonstrated that Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++, can effectively visualize the regions contributing to classification decisions, thereby increasing the trustworthiness of the model.
Grad-CAM++ provided superior explainability and better coverage of relevant regions compared to Grad-CAM, as validated against attribute masks from the ISIC 2018 dataset.

The proposed solution shows competitive performance compared to existing studies (e.g., InceptionV3, IM-CNN, VGG-16) while addressing the lack of explainability in many prior deep learning approaches for melanoma detection.

The authors acknowledge that the current evaluation of explainability relies on visual comparison with human eyesight and suggest future work should employ quantitative methods such as Intersection over Union (IoU) to evaluate heatmap images against segmented mask images.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (malignant melanoma vs. nevus) using a small, balanced subset of 1,228 images from the HAM10000 dataset, which may not reflect the class imbalance and diversity of the full dataset.

Explainability validation relies on visual comparison with human eyesight and Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) against attribute masks, rather than rigorous quantitative metrics like Intersection over Union (IoU), which the authors acknowledge as a limitation.

The dataset extraction excluded images with dark areas and noise (e.g., skin hair) to prevent incorrect classification, potentially introducing selection bias and limiting generalizability to real-world clinical scenarios with noisy inputs.

Data augmentation was restricted to rotation and flip only; other techniques were avoided to prevent the generation of false melanoma lesion structures, which may limit the model's robustness to other variations.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, relying instead on point estimates and comparisons with prior literature.
The model was evaluated on a single dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about generalizability to different populations or imaging devices.

The study is a conference paper with a relatively small sample size for deep learning, and the authors suggest future work should explore other architectures (e.g., transformers) and quantitative evaluation methods.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset (specifically from the ISIC 2018 Challenge Task 2) consisting of 1,228 images (614 nevus and 614 malignant melanoma). The dataset was split into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets. The model was trained on the training split, tuned using the validation split via Bayesian hyperparameter search, and evaluated on the testing split. The attribute masks from the ISIC 2018 Task 2 dataset were used to validate the explainability heatmaps, but this did not constitute a separate external test set for classification performance. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on subsets of the HAM10000 dataset. The attribute masks from ISIC 2018 Task 2 were used for explainability validation (visual comparison), not for classification performance testing on unseen data.

training.notes: Used for training the modified Xception model. Images were resized to 224x224 and augmented with rotation and flip.

validation.notes: Used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning via Bayesian search. The split ratio was 80:10:10.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. The split ratio was 80:10:10.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The Xception model was pre-trained on ImageNet weights before transfer learning was applied to the HAM10000 dataset.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation was applied to the training set using rotation and flip. No synthetic data generation was reported.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 80:10:10 split ratio for training, validation, and testing respectively. Total 1228 images (614 nevus, 614 malignant melanoma).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used for classification testing. The attribute masks from the ISIC 2018 Task 2 dataset were used to validate the explainability visualizations (heatmaps) against ground truth masks, but this was not a classification test set.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Modified Xception (Retrained with batch-normalization layer unfrozen)
dataset_level_results.notes: Best performing model selected based on accuracy. Evaluated on the 10% test split of the balanced HAM10000 subset.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Modified Xception (Retrained with batch-normalization layer frozen)
dataset_level_results.notes: Comparison model with frozen batch normalization layers. Lower performance than the unfrozen variant.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about generalizability to different populations or imaging devices.

The dataset extraction excluded images with dark areas and noise (e.g., skin hair) to prevent incorrect classification, potentially introducing selection bias and limiting generalizability to real-world clinical scenarios with noisy inputs.
The study used a small, balanced subset (1,228 images) of the HAM10000 dataset, which may not reflect the class imbalance and diversity of the full dataset.
Data augmentation was restricted to rotation and flip only, which may limit the model's robustness to other variations.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images in the training and validation splits (only the ratio 80:10:10 is given, though counts can be inferred as 982 and 123 respectively from the total 1228).
Patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient were split across train/test sets).
Confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
